title: Implement `takt upgrade` execution and summary flow
description: Implement `command_upgrade()` to execute the decision table, apply or preview file operations, update the manifest, merge config defaults, print per-file actions, and report the final summary with locally modified files.
acceptance_criteria: `takt upgrade` applies the spec decision table for bundled assets, including restore, new install, update, local-modification skip, user-owned skip, and removed-bundle disable behaviors.
`takt upgrade --dry-run` prints the same planned outcomes and config additions without writing files.
Config merge runs as part of upgrade, prints each added key, and preserves existing user values.
Successful upgrade returns exit code 0 even when files are intentionally skipped; only actual failures return non-zero.

handoff_summary.completed:
Implemented `takt upgrade` execution and summary flow:
- Asset decision table already existed (new, update, unchanged, restored, skipped_modified, skipped_user_owned, disabled, user_added)
- Dry-run mode already printed per-file actions without writing
- Added config key merge step: reads user config.yaml + bundled default, merges missing keys via `merge_config_keys()`, prints each added key, writes updated config (skipped in dry-run)
- Summary now includes `config-keys-added=N` count
- Return 0 is unconditional — skips do not cause failures
handoff_summary.remaining: None — all four acceptance criteria are satisfied.
handoff_summary.risks: YAML round-trip through `yaml.dump()` may reorder keys or change formatting in the user's config.yaml. This is an inherent trade-off of using PyYAML for config mutation. Users with carefully formatted configs may notice cosmetic differences after a key-adding upgrade.
handoff_summary.next_action: Tester agent should verify the upgrade flow end-to-end: asset decision table correctness, dry-run mode prints without writing, config merge adds missing keys, exit code 0 on skipped files.
handoff_summary.conflict_risks: None — changes are confined to `command_upgrade()` in `cli.py` (lines 1518–1551). No other bead should be touching this function.
handoff_summary.design_decisions: Config merge is placed after the manifest write and before the summary, so the summary can include `config-keys-added=N`. The YAML is read/written via `yaml.safe_load`/`yaml.dump` — a simple approach that works correctly but may reformat the user's config.yaml cosmetically. An alternative (ruamel.yaml with round-trip preservation) was considered but would add a new dependency for a marginal UX benefit. The `merge_config_keys()` helper already exists in `onboarding.py` (from bead B-91ecaadf) and is reused directly. Config additions are printed even in dry-run mode so users can preview what keys would be added.
handoff_summary.test_coverage_notes: Tester should verify: (1) `takt upgrade` applies each of the 8 action types correctly; (2) `--dry-run` prints all actions and config additions but writes nothing; (3) config merge inserts new bundled keys into a user config that is missing them, preserving existing values; (4) config merge is a no-op when user config already has all bundled keys; (5) exit code is 0 when files are skipped (modified/user-owned); (6) manifest `upgraded_at` timestamp is updated on a real run.
handoff_summary.known_limitations: If `.takt/config.yaml` does not exist (e.g., on a project that was never initialised), the config merge block is silently skipped. This is intentional — upgrade should not create a config from scratch (that is `takt init`'s job). YAML round-trip formatting changes are a known cosmetic limitation.

conflict_risks: None — changes are confined to `command_upgrade()` in `cli.py` (lines 1518–1551). No other bead should be touching this function.

execution_history.summary: Added config key merge step to `command_upgrade()` in `cli.py`. The existing implementation already handled the full asset decision table (new, update, unchanged, restored, skipped_modified, skipped_user_owned, disabled, user_added) and dry-run/manifest write flows. The missing piece was the config merge: after the manifest write, the function now loads `.takt/config.yaml` and the bundled default config, calls `merge_config_keys()` to insert any new keys absent from the user's config, prints each added key with a `[added]` label, and writes the updated config (skipped in dry-run mode). The summary line now also includes `config-keys-added=N`.

guardrails.template_text:
# Developer Guardrails

Primary responsibility: Implement only the assigned bead inside the existing architecture and file scope.

Allowed actions:
- Modify code, configuration, or local docs required to complete the assigned bead.
- Create sub-beads for discovered follow-up work that should be handled separately.
- Verify your `{{LANGUAGE}}` changes compile and import correctly with a quick syntax check: `{{BUILD_CHECK_COMMAND}}`.

Disallowed actions:
- Run any test suite — not even a targeted `{{TEST_COMMAND}}` run or any subset. Test execution is exclusively the tester agent's responsibility. Running tests wastes the agent budget and delays the pipeline.
- Redesign unrelated architecture or broaden scope beyond the assigned bead.
- Perform final review signoff in place of a review agent.
- Silently absorb unrelated test, documentation, or planning work that should be handed off.

Required Handoff Fields:

Every developer bead **must** populate the following three fields in its output JSON. Reviewers and tester agents rely on these fields to scope their work without additional back-and-forth turns. Use `"N/A"` only when a field is genuinely inapplicable (e.g., a trivial rename with no design trade-offs); do not leave fields blank.

- **`design_decisions`** — Document non-obvious architectural or implementation choices made during this bead. Include alternatives considered and why the chosen approach was preferred. Reviewers use this to evaluate correctness without re-deriving intent. Set to `"N/A"` only if the change was purely mechanical with no meaningful trade-offs.

- **`test_coverage_notes`** — Describe what the tester agent should verify: new code paths, edge cases introduced, regression risks, and any known untested scenarios left to the tester. Set to `"N/A"` only if the bead touched no executable code paths (e.g., documentation-only changes).

- **`known_limitations`** — Call out constraints, deferred work, or known gaps in the implementation. This includes out-of-scope items deliberately left for follow-up beads and any areas where the implementation is intentionally incomplete. Set to `"N/A"` if the implementation is complete as specified with no known gaps.

Expected outputs:
- Completed or blocked JSON with concise implementation summary.
- Accurate touched files, changed files, risks, and follow-up handoff fields.
- Populated `design_decisions`, `test_coverage_notes`, and `known_limitations` fields in every response.
